techniques.evidence_quote: The adversaries may have had about 13 good log-ins for Uber. They accessed these GitHub accounts intending to find AWS credentials.

They had a script developed through a freelancer on Upwork, which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub... 'Over a hundred thousand' successful log-ins were output by the adversary's script.
The testimony sounds like they would use simple GitHub searches to surface these credentials... The 2016 incident involved a credential that had existed without rotation since 2013.
They succeeded in finding several AWS credentials and loaded them into a tool called S3 Browser. From here, the adversary discovered hourly encrypted backups.

After a day or so, the third person indicated to the adversaries that there was an 'interesting' object... These database objects were created outside the regular automated backup... an engineer created backups while decommissioning a service, writing them without encryption.
One of the adversaries proceeded to download on 2014–05–12. This was a database containing 50 million records.

'I have found a major vulnerability in Uber. I was able to dump Uber database and many other things.'... The adversary rejected Uber's $10K counter with a demand in the six figures... Uber instructed HackerOne to release the payment shortly after, on 2016–12–08.

attack_chain.description: Attackers acquired a large database of over 10 million username/password pairs sourced from other breaches, trades, and public leaks, used to fuel a credential stuffing campaign.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They describe this database as having 'over ten million' entries.

attack_chain.description: Attackers ran a custom Upwork-developed script that ingested the credential database and attempted to authenticate against GitHub, yielding over 100,000 successful logins, approximately 13 of which were associated with Uber GitHub organizations.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They had a script developed through a freelancer on Upwork, which would ingest emails and passwords and attempt to log into GitHub... 'Over a hundred thousand' successful log-ins were output by the adversary's script.

attack_chain.description: Using valid, compromised GitHub credentials for approximately 13 Uber-affiliated accounts, attackers authenticated to GitHub and gained access to Uber's private repositories.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The adversaries may have had about 13 good log-ins for Uber. They accessed these GitHub accounts intending to find AWS credentials.

attack_chain.description: Attackers searched private GitHub repositories for hardcoded AWS IAM credentials. They located a long-lived credential (codenamed 'genghis') dating to 2013 that had never been rotated, embedded in source code.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The testimony sounds like they would use simple GitHub searches to surface these credentials... The 2016 incident involved a credential that had existed without rotation since 2013.

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen AWS IAM credentials loaded into 'S3 Browser', attackers enumerated available S3 buckets, discovering the 'cold-storage' bucket containing hourly database backups.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They succeeded in finding several AWS credentials and loaded them into a tool called S3 Browser. From here, the adversary discovered hourly encrypted backups.

attack_chain.description: Attackers enumerated objects within the cold-storage S3 bucket. Most backups were encrypted and inaccessible, but a third-party collaborator identified a specific unencrypted plaintext backup object created manually outside the normal automated pipeline.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: After a day or so, the third person indicated to the adversaries that there was an 'interesting' object... These database objects were created outside the regular automated backup... an engineer created backups while decommissioning a service, writing them without encryption.

attack_chain.description: The attacker downloaded the unencrypted plaintext database backup from the S3 cold-storage bucket. This object contained approximately 50 million user records and driver information including ~600,000 driver's license numbers.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: One of the adversaries proceeded to download on 2014–05–12. This was a database containing 50 million records.

attack_chain.description: After exfiltrating data, the attackers contacted Uber via ProtonMail claiming to have found a 'major vulnerability' and dump of data, demanding payment in the high six figures in an extortion scheme. Uber ultimately paid $100,000 through HackerOne.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: 'I have found a major vulnerability in Uber. I was able to dump Uber database and many other things.'... The adversary rejected Uber's $10K counter with a demand in the six figures... Uber instructed HackerOne to release the payment shortly after, on 2016–12–08.

initial_access_vector: Credential stuffing attack using a database of 10M+ leaked credentials against GitHub; ~13 valid Uber employee GitHub logins obtained, granting access to private repositories containing hardcoded AWS IAM keys.
persistence_mechanism: None identified — attackers used a static, never-rotated AWS IAM credential ('genghis', created 2013) that remained valid throughout the attack window due to absence of credential rotation policies.
privilege_escalation: No explicit privilege escalation described; the stolen AWS IAM credential ('genghis') had sufficient permissions by default to access the S3 cold-storage bucket and exfiltrate data remotely without further escalation.
